framing: Every candidate scored distribution-first: the first-ten-customers path is the heaviest-weighted axis, and any candidate that cannot name a credible one scores down automatically (mission rule). Scores are a weighted 0–5 blend.

axes.name: Distribution
axes.measures: Credibility + timeliness of the first-ten-customers path. Human-sales-gated paths (agents can't own the channel) score down.
axes.question: Is there a named, reachable surface where buyers with this pain already look — and can YOU reach it?
axes.plain: Where will the first ten customers actually come from? Most side projects die here, which is why this axis alone is worth more than a third of the total.

anchors.text: an owned or proven channel with demonstrated pull (an audience, a ranking page, a marketplace category you already convert in).
a real, named channel exists but is crowded, low-intent, or gated by someone else's approval (communities with self-promo rules, directories).
“I'll post it and people will find it.”

axes.name: Agent-buildability
axes.measures: Share of the work agents can do with zero owner involvement.
axes.question: Can you ship a real v0.1 with the skills/tools you have, without new accounts, hires, or unknowns?
axes.plain: How much of the build can be done with what you already have — no new skills, accounts, or paid services standing between you and version 0.1.

anchors.text: fully buildable from what you already have; no new accounts or paid services needed for v0.1.
buildable with one or two learnable unknowns or minor dependencies.
depends on a skill, dataset, or access you do not have today.

axes.name: Owner-click cost
axes.measures: Fewer, one-time clicks beat recurring owner labor (ongoing sales/outreach is a recurring cost).
axes.question: How many manual, gated, or high-friction steps stand between “built” and “purchasable”?
axes.plain: The launch friction: one-time publish clicks are cheap; recurring human work (sales calls, outreach, support) is a cost you pay forever.

anchors.text: one publish click on an existing storefront.
several manual steps (listings, submissions, payout setup).
approvals, reviews, compliance, or partner sign-off before day one.

axes.name: Speed to first dollar
axes.measures: Calendar time to a plausible first payment.
axes.question: How soon after starting could the FIRST dollar arrive (not scale — the first dollar)?
axes.plain: Not how big it could get — how soon the very first payment could plausibly land.

anchors.text: sellable after a build measured in weeks, or dependent on an upstream artifact shipping first.
revenue only after traction on a free layer (funnel-first plays).

axes.name: WTP / moat
axes.question: Will this audience actually PAY, and is there anything a free substitute can't copy in an afternoon?
axes.plain: Will anyone pay, and will they keep paying once a free alternative shows up? Advice, templates, and calculators with abundant free substitutes score low.

anchors.text: buyers already pay for worse versions of this; switching or DIY is genuinely painful.
narrow but real pain; few alternatives for those who have it.
advice, templates, or calculators with abundant free substitutes.

bands.label: do not build without a named exception in writing
borderline — only with a tight budget
best available candidate
bands.caveat: which is still not a promise anyone will buy it

reading_note: There is no magic pass mark. The total ranks candidates against each other; the kill-rule fields (deadline, path, budget) decide when a built one dies.
anti_gaming: Score distribution first, before you're attached to the total.
Rationale is mandatory per axis — a score without a sentence is a feeling, not a score.
The two lowest scores are the honest ones. Copy them into “Why this might fail” in plain words.
Show the arithmetic in the total row so a reviewer can recompute it.
Never tune weights to save a candidate (Kill Rule 4).
